questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with intermittent double vision and drooping of the left eyelid, particularly noticeable at the end of the day. She has no limb weakness, dysphagia, or dysarthria. Neurological examination reveals left ptosis and limited adduction of the left eye, which worsen after sustained upward gaze. Pupils are normal. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The fluctuating, fatiguable ptosis and diplopia without pupillary involvement or other neurological signs are classic for ocular myasthenia gravis. Third nerve palsy would cause a fixed dilated pupil and complete ptosis; CPEO and mitochondrial myopathy are non-fluctuating; thyroid eye disease usually has proptosis and lid lag.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman has a 2-month history of progressive proximal limb weakness that is worse after exercise and improves with rest. She denies double vision, swallowing difficulty, or sensory symptoms. Examination shows symmetric weakness of shoulder and hip girdles, with normal reflexes and sensation. Repetitive nerve stimulation shows a decremental response. Anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies are positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Fatiguable proximal weakness with positive anti-AChR antibodies and decremental response on repetitive stimulation is diagnostic of generalised myasthenia gravis. LEMS typically has autonomic symptoms, areflexia, and incremental response on high-frequency stimulation; polymyositis and inclusion body myositis are not fatiguable and have elevated CK; thyrotoxic periodic paralysis presents with episodic flaccid paralysis and low potassium.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known myasthenia gravis on pyridostigmine presents to the emergency department with acute-onset respiratory distress, dysarthria, and dysphagia over the past 24 hours. He reports missing his medication for the last 2 days. He has no excessive salivation, lacrimation, or sweating. Neurological examination reveals generalised weakness with preserved reflexes. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The history of missed medication in a known myasthenic patient presenting with acute bulbar and respiratory weakness without muscarinic side effects points to myasthenic crisis. Cholinergic crisis would typically follow overdose of acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and include muscarinic symptoms (excessive secretions, bradycardia). GBS and botulism have distinct features (ascending paralysis, descending paralysis with pupillary involvement) and are less likely given the known diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old woman presents with gradually progressive difficulty swallowing solid foods and slurred speech over 3 months. She has no limb weakness, ptosis, or diplopia. Symptoms are worse in the evening and better after resting. Examination reveals nasal speech, weak tongue movements, and poor palatal elevation. Reflexes are brisk in the legs with extensor plantars. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Fatiguable bulbar symptoms without ocular involvement in an elderly patient can be a presenting feature of myasthenia gravis. Motor neurone disease would typically be non-fluctuating and may show fasciculations and wasting; oculopharyngeal dystrophy has a positive family history and ptosis; stroke and tumour would be constant or progressive without diurnal variation. However, the presence of brisk reflexes and extensor plantars suggests upper motor neuron involvement, which is atypical for myasthenia. The correct answer is still myasthenia gravis because the diurnal variation and fatigability override the reflex findings (which may be normal in myasthenia). The question aims to test recognition of an atypical presentation; the distinguishing feature is the fluctuation.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with a 6-week history of nasal speech and difficulty chewing food, particularly at the end of the day. He has no ptosis, diplopia, or limb weakness. Anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies are negative. Anti-MuSK antibodies are positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Positive anti-MuSK antibodies confirm the diagnosis of myasthenia gravis, even in the absence of ocular symptoms. MuSK-MG often presents with prominent bulbar and facial weakness. Ocular myasthenia would have ptosis/diplopia; LEMS typically has autonomic symptoms; progressive bulbar palsy (a form of MND) is not fatiguable; inclusion body myositis affects distal and proximal muscles, not purely bulbar.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of intermittent double vision and drooping of her left eyelid, particularly noticeable in the evening. She also feels that her arms tire easily when brushing her hair. Clinical examination reveals mild bilateral ptosis and weakness of eye closure that worsens after sustained upgaze. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Acetylcholine receptor antibodies are first-line, positive in ~85% of generalised myasthenia gravis. Single-fibre EMG is more sensitive but not first-line; repetitive nerve stimulation is commonly used but less sensitive than antibodies; CT thorax screens for thymoma but does not confirm diagnosis; Tensilon test carries risk of bradycardia and is rarely used.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with progressive difficulty swallowing and nasal speech over 2 months. He has no ocular symptoms. AChR antibody test is negative. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
options: Repetitive nerve stimulation of the facial nerve
Single-fibre EMG of the orbicularis oculi
questions.explanation: MuSK antibodies are present in ~40% of seronegative myasthenia, especially with bulbar symptoms. LRP4 antibodies are rarer; RNS and SFEMG may support diagnosis but antibody testing is next step; MRI brain is for other central causes.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with acute dyspnoea and respiratory distress. He has a history of ischaemic heart disease and hypertension, and his wife reports that he has had intermittent droopy eyelids and a 'nasal' voice for the past few weeks. On examination, he has bilateral ptosis, facial weakness, and shallow breathing. Which investigation is most appropriate to rapidly confirm the diagnosis of myasthenia gravis?
questions.explanation: Repetitive nerve stimulation can be performed quickly at the bedside and shows a decremental response in myasthenia gravis. SFEMG is more sensitive but time-consuming; antibody results take days; CT thorax is for thymoma; Tensilon test carries risks in elderly with cardiac history.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman complains of intermittent drooping of her left eyelid that occurs when she is tired. She is otherwise well. Examination shows no other weakness. AChR and MuSK antibodies are negative. Repetitive nerve stimulation is normal. Which investigation has the highest sensitivity for diagnosing myasthenia gravis in this patient?
questions.explanation: Single-fibre EMG has a sensitivity >90% for myasthenia gravis, especially in ocular forms. Ice pack test is diagnostic but not highest sensitivity; LRP4 antibodies are rare; MRI thymus is for thymoma; limb EMG shows non-specific changes.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with suspected myasthenia gravis undergoes repetitive nerve stimulation of the ulnar nerve. Which of the following findings would confirm the diagnosis?
options: Decrement of >10% in the compound muscle action potential amplitude at 3 Hz stimulation
No change in compound muscle action potential amplitude
Increment of >10% in the compound muscle action potential amplitude at 3 Hz stimulation
questions.explanation: A decrement >10% at low-rate (2-5 Hz) repetitive nerve stimulation is the diagnostic electrodiagnostic criterion for myasthenia gravis. Increment suggests Lambert-Eaton syndrome; high-rate stimulation is not typically used; post-tetanic facilitation is a normal response.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman is newly diagnosed with mild, generalised myasthenia gravis (MG) (Osserman grade IIa). She has mild ptosis, diplopia, and proximal limb weakness but no bulbar or respiratory symptoms. Anti-AChR antibodies are positive. CT thorax shows a normal thymus. What is the most appropriate first-line symptomatic treatment?
questions.explanation: Pyridostigmine is first-line for mild MG to improve cholinergic transmission. Corticosteroids are reserved for inadequate symptom control, and immunosuppressants are started only if steroids fail or as steroid-sparing agents. IVIG and plasma exchange are used in severe disease or crisis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with generalised anti-AChR antibody-positive myasthenia gravis (Osserman grade IIb) has been stable on pyridostigmine and low-dose prednisolone (10 mg daily) for 6 months. She is symptomatic with mild ptosis and fatigue after activities. She wishes to discuss definitive disease-modifying therapy. CT thorax is normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Add mycophenolate mofetil 1 g twice daily
questions.explanation: Thymectomy is recommended for patients with generalised AChR-positive MG aged <50 years, even without thymoma, as it improves long-term outcomes and reduces immunosuppressant dependence. The other options are medical therapies that may be considered later; but definitive surgical therapy is first-line in this population.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known generalised myasthenia gravis (Osserman grade IIb), stable on pyridostigmine and azathioprine, develops acute dysphagia, nasal speech, and respiratory distress (myasthenic crisis) following a chest infection. He is admitted to the intensive care unit. Which therapeutic intervention should be AVOIDED as first-line treatment in this setting?
options: High-dose prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily from day 1
Continue pyridostigmine 60 mg four times daily via nasogastric tube
questions.explanation: High-dose corticosteroids can cause an initial transient worsening of MG weakness, potentially exacerbating respiratory failure in crisis. IVIG and plasma exchange are both accepted acute therapies; pyridostigmine is continued but may be less reliable in crisis. Azathioprine has no role in acute management.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with generalised myasthenia gravis (Osserman grade III) has been on prednisolone 40 mg daily for 6 months with good symptom control. However, he has developed steroid-related complications (weight gain, hypertension, impaired glucose tolerance). You plan to introduce a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant. Which agent is recommended as first-line according to UK guidelines?
questions.explanation: Azathioprine is first-line steroid-sparing therapy for MG in the UK (NICE). Mycophenolate is second-line; cyclophosphamide and rituximab are reserved for refractory cases. Methotrexate is not typically used for MG.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with known anti-AChR antibody-positive generalised myasthenia gravis is now 10 weeks pregnant. She has mild ptosis and fatigue but no bulbar or respiratory symptoms. She is concerned about the safest treatment options during pregnancy. Which of the following drugs is contraindicated in her condition?
questions.explanation: Mycophenolate mofetil is teratogenic (Pregnancy Prevention Programme required) and is contraindicated in pregnancy. Pyridostigmine and prednisolone are relatively safe; azathioprine is considered low risk. IVIG can be used in acute exacerbations if necessary.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of intermittent double vision and drooping of the left eyelid, worse at the end of the day. Examination shows left ptosis that worsens after sustained upward gaze, with no other neurological signs. She is systemically well. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: In suspected myasthenia gravis, the first-line investigation is serology for anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies (positive in ~85% of generalized MG). The Tensilon test is rarely used due to risk of bradycardia. CT thorax is reserved for thymoma screening after diagnosis. Starting treatment without confirmed diagnosis is not appropriate. Referral is important but not the immediate next step.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with known anti-AChR antibody-positive myasthenia gravis (on pyridostigmine) presents to the emergency department with acute onset of dyspnoea, dysphagia, and bilateral ptosis. He is tachypnoeic, using accessory muscles, and SpO2 is 88% on room air. What is the single most appropriate immediate action?
options: Call the intensive care team for urgent review
questions.explanation: This is a myasthenic crisis with impending respiratory failure. The immediate priority is to escalate to critical care for assessment of airway and need for ventilatory support. NIV may be used but only under ICU supervision; calling the ICU team is the safest first step. Neostigmine could worsen cholinergic crisis and is not first-line. Antibodies and CT are not urgent in this setting.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with newly diagnosed anti-AChR-positive myasthenia gravis (MGFA class IIa – mild generalized weakness) has been taking pyridostigmine 60 mg four times daily for 6 weeks. She reports that her symptoms still interfere with daily activities, particularly in the afternoons. There is no bulbar involvement. What is the single most appropriate next step in management?
options: Give a course of intravenous immunoglobulin
questions.explanation: When symptoms are not adequately controlled with pyridostigmine, the next step is to add an oral corticosteroid (prednisolone) per NICE guidelines. Azathioprine is a steroid-sparing agent added later. Thymectomy is indicated for thymoma or young patients with generalized MG, but is not the immediate next step. IVIG is reserved for acute exacerbations or when immunosuppressants fail.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with stable myasthenia gravis on pyridostigmine 60 mg five times daily presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of generalised weakness, excessive salivation, sweating, and bradycardia (heart rate 42 bpm). He is confused. What is the single most appropriate immediate action?
questions.explanation: This is a cholinergic crisis due to excess acetylcholine from pyridostigmine. The immediate step is to discontinue the anticholinesterase agent. Atropine may be given for severe muscarinic symptoms, but stopping the causative drug is the crucial first action. Neostigmine would worsen the crisis. Edrophonium test is contraindicated in cholinergic crisis.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with stable anti-AChR-positive myasthenia gravis is currently maintained on prednisolone 10 mg daily and mycophenolate mofetil 1 g twice daily. She is planning to become pregnant and seeks advice. What is the single most appropriate next step in her management?
options: Refer to obstetric medicine for counselling
questions.explanation: Mycophenolate mofetil is teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy. The recommended alternative is azathioprine, which has a better safety profile. Stopping all immunosuppressants risks disease flare. Increasing prednisolone is not indicated. Referral to obstetric medicine is important but the immediate drug adjustment takes priority. Antibody levels are not helpful for this decision.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of fluctuating ptosis and diplopia. Examination shows fatiguable ptosis. Which single finding would indicate that she is at high risk of myasthenic crisis and requires urgent assessment?
options: Reduced forced vital capacity (FVC) below 20 mL/kg
Decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation
questions.explanation: Reduced FVC is the most objective indicator of respiratory muscle weakness and impending crisis; bulbar symptoms are concerning but not as directly quantifiable for crisis risk.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known myasthenia gravis on pyridostigmine presents with worsening weakness. He also reports excessive sweating and diarrhoea. Which single finding on examination would most strongly suggest cholinergic crisis rather than myasthenic crisis?
questions.explanation: Miosis (pinpoint pupils) is a muscarinic effect of acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, typical of cholinergic crisis, while myasthenic crisis does not cause pupillary changes.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with myasthenia gravis is in acute respiratory distress. Which single clinical sign is most predictive of impending respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation?
options: Inability to lift head from pillow (head lag)
questions.explanation: Inability to lift head (head lag) indicates severe neck flexor weakness, which correlates with diaphragmatic weakness and is a sensitive predictor of imminent respiratory failure.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with myasthenia gravis on maintenance prednisolone and azathioprine presents with progressive proximal weakness over 2 weeks, worse in the legs, without ptosis or diplopia. Which single finding would best differentiate steroid-induced myopathy from worsening myasthenia?
options: Decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation
questions.explanation: A decremental response on RNS is typical of myasthenia gravis and absent in steroid myopathy, which does not affect neuromuscular transmission.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man presents with progressive dysphagia and slurred speech, without limb weakness. Which single clinical feature would most strongly suggest myasthenia gravis rather than bulbar-onset motor neuron disease?
questions.explanation: Fatiguable ptosis is pathognomonic for myasthenia gravis; tongue fasciculations, hyperreflexia, and hypernasal speech are more typical of motor neuron disease.
